steps.title: Analyze f(x) for a maximum
steps.content: $f(x) = 20(1.18)^x + 38$ has base $1.18 \gt  1$, so it grows without bound as $x$ increases. For $x \ge 0$ there is no maximum value. The constant $38$ is not the maximum (at $x = 0$, $f(0) = 20 + 38 = 58$); it is simply a vertical shift. So $f$ does not display its maximum as a constant or coefficient.

steps.title: Analyze g(x) for a maximum
steps.content:
$g(x) = 7(0.68)^x$ has base $0.68$ where $0 \lt 0.68 \lt 1$, so it decays toward $0$ as $x$ increases. For $x \ge 0$ the maximum occurs at $x = 0$:
$$g(0) = 7(0.68)^0 = 7 \cdot 1 = 7$$
The coefficient $7$ in the equation is exactly the maximum value. So $g$ does display its maximum as a coefficient.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice
steps.content:
• A) I only: $f$ has no maximum on $x \ge 0$, so it cannot display one. Eliminated.
  • B) II only: Only $g$ displays its maximum (the coefficient $7$). Matches.
  • C) I and II: $f$ does not display a maximum. Eliminated.
  • D) Neither I nor II: $g$ does display its maximum. Eliminated.
That matches the correct answer — B is correct.
